risk_description: EU Forced Labour Regulation (applicable Dec 2027) will prohibit EU market access for any goods containing electronics produced with forced labour from China. No size-of-company exemption applies. Market withdrawal and destruction orders are the enforcement mechanism. All EU-destined supply chains sourcing electronics from China require verified forced-labour-free traceability by December 2027.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU Forced Labour Regulation (EU) 2024/3015 — Applicable December 2027
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal — Regulation (EU) 2024/3015; European Commission Guidance
context: EU Forced Labour Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2024/3015) was published November 2024 and will apply from December 2027. It prohibits placing on the EU market any goods produced with forced labour, regardless of origin. Unlike CSDDD, it applies to ALL goods and ALL companies (no size threshold). Competent national authorities will investigate and order market withdrawal and destruction of non-compliant goods. This is the most significant new social risk regulatory framework for EU-destined supply chains within the 5-year horizon.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU Commission implementing regulations (product and country prioritisation lists expected 2025-2026); national competent authority designation; EU Forced Labour Single Entry Point portal; industry guidance from ETOA, amfori, ecovadis on compliance programme design

risk_description: EU Forced Labour Regulation will apply to Vietnamese-origin rubber from December 2027. EU CSDDD due diligence obligations for Vietnam-sourced inputs are coming into force concurrently. Labour conditions in Vietnam's plantation and processing sectors require verified compliance documentation.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU Forced Labour Regulation (EU) 2024/3015 — Vietnam Sourcing Risk
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal — Regulation (EU) 2024/3015; ILO Better Work Vietnam
context: Vietnam's growing role as a China+1 manufacturing hub means an increasing share of EU-bound goods originate from Vietnam. EU Forced Labour Regulation applies to Vietnamese goods from Dec 2027. CSDDD will require due diligence on Vietnamese manufacturing labour conditions. ILO Better Work Vietnam programme documents ongoing issues in textiles, electronics assembly, and seafood sectors.
monitoring_signals: Monitor ILO Better Work Vietnam reports; EU Forced Labour Regulation country risk assessments (expected from EU Commission 2025-2026); US State Dept Vietnam TIP Report; ILAB Vietnam TVPRA status

risk_level: Possible within 5 years — India sourcing share increasing
risk_description: India's growing electronics manufacturing exports are bringing ILAB and EU CSDDD scrutiny to Indian electronics assembly labour conditions. Supply chains diversifying from China to India may encounter new social risk exposure within the 5-year horizon as Indian electronics output scales and ILAB attention grows.
primary_restriction_instrument: ILAB TVPRA Monitoring — India Electronics Sector; EU CSDDD
secondary_restriction_instrument: ILAB India Country Reports; ILO India Electronics Sector; EU CSDDD India Exposure Assessment
context: India's electronics manufacturing sector is growing rapidly under the Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme, with smartphone and component output increasing 40%+ year-on-year 2022-2025. As India becomes a major electronics exporter (particularly to USA and EU), ILAB and EU CSDDD scrutiny will increase. ZURU and Garmin supply chains may increasingly have Indian electronics component inputs over the 5-year horizon. ILO reports document contract labour, excessive overtime, and gender discrimination in Indian electronics assembly.
monitoring_signals: Monitor ILO India electronics sector reports; ILAB India country report annual updates; EU Commission India partnership framework labour provisions; India SECC electronics worker welfare data; Know The Chain benchmark scores

risk_description: EU CSDDD and EU Forced Labour Regulation (Dec 2027) will require due diligence on cotton supply chains from India. ILAB documents child and forced labour in Indian cotton farming and textile production. Supply chains with Indian cotton inputs will require verified compliance documentation for EU market access from 2027.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU Forced Labour Regulation (EU) 2024/3015; EU CSDDD; ILAB TVPRA Monitoring
secondary_restriction_instrument: ILAB 2024 TVPRA List; ILO India Cotton Sector; EU CSDDD; EU FL Regulation (EU) 2024/3015
context: India is one of the world's largest cotton and garment producers. EU CSDDD will require supply chain due diligence for Indian cotton and garment supply chains by 2027-2028. ILAB and ILO document child labour in cotton farming (especially hybrid cottonseed) and forced overtime in garment factories. The EU Forced Labour Regulation will apply from Dec 2027, creating a significant compliance burden for supply chains with Indian cotton inputs.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU CSDDD transposition for textile sector; ILAB India cotton and garment sector reports; Fair Labor Association India audit data; EU Forced Labour Regulation country risk list for India

risk_level: Likely within 5 years — structural enforcement trajectory clear
risk_description: UFLPA structural expansion over the 5-year horizon is expected to broaden enforcement scope on Chinese-origin electronics. New entity list additions, expanded sector coverage, and potential multi-jurisdiction enforcement (UK, Canada, Australia following US model) will increase compliance burden significantly. Supply chain tracing requirements will become more stringent.
primary_restriction_instrument: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA) — Structural Enforcement Expansion
secondary_restriction_instrument: CECC Annual Report; UFLPA Strategic Enforcement Plan; US CBP Trade Statistics
context: Congressional and executive branch signals indicate UFLPA enforcement will expand structurally over the 5-year horizon. Potential developments include: extension of rebuttable presumption to new sectors beyond Xinjiang; new entity list additions at accelerating pace; potential mirroring legislation in UK, Canada, and Australia creating a multi-jurisdiction forced labour import bar. The trajectory is clearly toward stricter enforcement and broader product coverage.
monitoring_signals: Monitor CECC annual China reports; UFLPA Strategic Enforcement Plan updates; US Senate Finance Committee and House Ways & Means trade hearings; UK Modern Slavery Act reform proposals; Canadian SCOFA Act progress

risk_level: Likely within 5 years — structural enforcement trajectory clear

risk_description: EU EUDR full enforcement on Brazilian cattle will be in place within the 5-year horizon. EU CSDDD additionally requires active human rights due diligence. Supply chain traceability to farm/operation level will be a mandatory requirement for EU market access, significantly raising compliance costs and creating sourcing disruption risk.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) 2023/1115 — Full Enforcement; EU CSDDD
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal 2023/1115; EU CSDDD 2024/1760; Global Forest Watch
context: Brazil EUDR full enforcement will be in place by 2026. Beyond cattle and cocoa, timber and coffee are the next highest-risk Brazilian commodities under EUDR. EU CSDDD will additionally require active human rights due diligence on Brazilian agricultural supply chains by 2027. Brazil's deforestation rate in the Cerrado (outside PRODES/DETER monitoring scope) remains a significant compliance risk area.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EUDR implementing regulation for Cerrado coverage; Brazilian SOJA PLUS and Roundtable on Responsible Soy compliance programmes; MapBiomas Cerrado deforestation data; EU Commission EUDR enforcement reports

risk_level: Compliance obligation from 2027 — restriction requires formal EU FL Reg investigation
risk_description: Sugarcane is not an EUDR commodity. The 5-year restriction pathway runs through EU CSDDD (mandatory remediation of supply chain labour abuses) and, as a tail risk, the EU Forced Labour Regulation (Dec 2027), which could prohibit EU market access for goods if a formal investigation finds forced labour in the Brazilian sugarcane supply chain. The CSDDD obligation is certain; the Forced Labour Regulation restriction requires an active investigation and finding — plausible but not assured within 5 years.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU CSDDD 2024/1760; EU Forced Labour Regulation (EU) 2024/3015 — Tail Risk
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal 2023/1115; EU CSDDD 2024/1760; Global Forest Watch
context: Brazil sugarcane sector is ILAB-listed for child labour. EU CSDDD will require active remediation of supply chain labour abuses. EU Forced Labour Regulation (applicable Dec 2027) creates a potential market access prohibition pathway IF a formal investigation is opened and sustained. However, sugarcane is not an EUDR commodity, so automatic traceability requirements do not apply. Confidence is moderate for compliance obligation; low for outright restriction.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EUDR implementing regulation for Cerrado coverage; Brazilian SOJA PLUS and Roundtable on Responsible Soy compliance programmes; MapBiomas Cerrado deforestation data; EU Commission EUDR enforcement reports

risk_level: Likely within 5 years — 2026 review mandated under treaty
risk_description: USMCA 2026 review may tighten labour enforcement provisions affecting Mexican cattle supply chains. Mexico nearshoring scale-up is drawing increased ILAB and ILO scrutiny to manufacturing zone labour conditions. Compliance programmes must be updated ahead of the review.
primary_restriction_instrument: USMCA Chapter 23 Labour Provisions — 2026 Joint Review
secondary_restriction_instrument: USTR USMCA 2026 Review; ILAB Mexico; ILO Better Work Mexico
context: The 2026 USMCA joint review will assess implementation of labour provisions. Mexico's nearshoring boom (driven by US-China decoupling) is concentrating manufacturing labour in new industrial corridors (Monterrey, Saltillo, Querétaro). USMCA Rapid Response Mechanism may be applied to more facilities as the manufacturing base expands. ILO, ILAB, and NGOs are increasing scrutiny of labour conditions in new manufacturing zones.
monitoring_signals: Monitor USTR 2026 USMCA review process; ILAB Mexico country report updates; Mexican STPS labour reform implementation data; ILO Better Work Mexico outcomes; USMCA Rapid Response Mechanism petition filings

risk_level: Compliance obligation likely — direct restriction is low-probability tail risk within 5 years
risk_description: EU CSDDD full enforcement by 2027-2028 will require documented due diligence on South African coal supply chains used in steel production for EU-bound goods. CBAM applies to embedded carbon content and is not a labour restriction instrument. The restriction pathway to actual goods prohibition requires: CSDDD non-compliance finding → escalation to EU Forced Labour Regulation investigation → market withdrawal order. This is a multi-step pathway with genuine uncertainty at each stage. Flagged as a compliance monitoring priority, not a near-term restriction signal.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU CSDDD 2024/1760; EU Forced Labour Regulation (EU) 2024/3015 — Tail Risk
secondary_restriction_instrument: SAHRC Mining Reports; ILO South Africa; EU CSDDD; EU FL Reg 2024/3015
context: South African coal mining labour conditions may trigger CSDDD due diligence obligations for EU-destined supply chains using South African coal-derived steel inputs. The direct restriction pathway under EU Forced Labour Regulation requires a formal investigation and finding of forced labour, which is a multi-year process dependent on NGO/competent authority trigger. CBAM should not be cited as a labour restriction mechanism — it prices carbon, not labour conditions. Confidence in actual restriction within 5 years is low; compliance obligation is real.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU CBAM registry; South African MPRDA compliance reports; SAHRC mining sector investigations; NUM/AMCU collective bargaining outcomes; EU Commission CSDDD South Africa sector guidance

risk_description: EU Forced Labour Regulation (Dec 2027) will require Garmin to verify that electronics assembled at Taiwan sites do not contain forced-labour-produced components anywhere in the supply chain. EU CSDDD additionally requires active human rights due diligence on the full supply chain. Component traceability programmes must be in place by 2027.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU Forced Labour Regulation (EU) 2024/3015; EU CSDDD — Electronics Supply Chain
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal — Regulation (EU) 2024/3015; EU CSDDD 2024/1760
context: EU Forced Labour Regulation (applicable Dec 2027) will apply to electronics assembled in Taiwan if they contain forced-labour-produced components from China. Unlike US UFLPA (which has focused on XUAR specifically), the EU FL Reg applies to all forced labour, anywhere in the supply chain. Garmin Taiwan operations will need to verify their entire component supply chain against forced labour standards for EU market access from December 2027.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU Forced Labour Regulation country risk assessments; EU Commission 'single entry point' portal for forced labour investigations; Garmin supplier audit programme outcomes; EU CSDDD Garmin compliance programme documentation

risk_level: Likely within 5 years — EUDR enforcement ramp-up and country benchmarking in progress
risk_description: EUDR full enforcement and EU Commission country benchmarking within 5 years creates a material risk that Argentine cattle supply chains are classified as high-risk, requiring enhanced due diligence or facing EU market access restrictions. Argentine beef used in SC Johnson soap and surfactant formulations will require farm-level deforestation-free certification, which Argentina's current traceability infrastructure cannot reliably provide. This is a direct goods restriction mechanism under EUDR — not a compliance obligation analogue.
primary_restriction_instrument: EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) 2023/1115 — Country Benchmarking / Argentine Cattle
secondary_restriction_instrument: EU Official Journal 2023/1115; Global Forest Watch Gran Chaco; Trase.earth; WWF Chaco Report 2024
context: Within 5 years, EU EUDR full enforcement on Argentine cattle is expected to be operational and increasingly effective. The European Commission is developing country benchmarking systems that will assess Argentina's national deforestation monitoring infrastructure. The Gran Chaco deforestation rate and Argentina's lack of farm-level cattle traceability comparable to Brazil's GTA/SISBOV system means Argentine beef is at structurally higher EUDR compliance risk than Brazilian beef. Within 5 years, enhanced EUDR enforcement and potential "high scrutiny" country classification for Argentina could create systematic EU market access barriers for Argentine cattle and beef products without verified deforestation-free provenance.
monitoring_signals: Monitor EU Commission EUDR country benchmarking classification for Argentina; Trase.earth Gran Chaco cattle deforestation exposure data; Argentine SENASA cattle traceability system improvements; EUDR competent authority enforcement case precedents for South American beef
